text:
Audio: Good morning, data 100 welcome to the largest upper division course at UC Berkeley. This is very exciting. Before we get started, I would like to ask everyone to either find a seat or please leave the lecture hall because of the fire code. And you should be able to join over Zoom and then watch us synchronously with us. And maybe next time we'll try to find a way to accommodate most people. There are some seats over here, there's some in the back, and I see like a cluster of small number of seats here. So either find a seat or please leave the lecture, and I'm sorry about that. This is kind of one part of the perk of having a large force, and this is the largest kind of lecture hall that we could get. All right. So you saw few QR codes that was posted online. Please go ahead. Scan this QR code. This is a Slido platform that we're using for kind of doing lecture attendance. If you're on your phone, scan it. If you're on your laptop, just go to Slido.com and then type in those digits. And you should be able to get in and then see all the polls that were posting. This is also where you can ask questions. And our wonderful TA's are here for responding to questions that you might have in kind of in a synchronous way. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course C >

Audio: We're going to see a quick demo and then you'll get a sense of what are the students that We have in data 100 and kind of see the majors, see the names and whatnot, and the data is kind of anonymized, but we'll be able to see a fun demo. And we're gonna go over some non-kind of fun things that we're gonna go over some logistics, and we'll read the syllabus, at least part of the syllabus together. So before I hand it over to Joey, let me introduce myself. My name is Nara Gessner, who is the assistant teaching professor in the EECS department. I joined Berkeley in 2012 before that I was at Santa Cruz as a faculty member since 2017. And before that, I did my PhD at University of Toronto in artificial perception lab. So my training is mostly in a statistical signal processing applied to machine learning and in biomedical domain. Since I joined Berkeley, I switched years a little bit and now I'm more focused on intelligent AI tutoring systems and how we can incorporate AI in education. So this semester I'm teaching data 100. This is the first time that I'm teaching the course. And then I'm also teaching to other research seminar course in EES, yes. So that's what I'll be doing. And then what I have is also that I hold office hours, both in my office and also in Barn Hall. So I'm looking forward to meeting you all in either of the office hours to chat and get to know you all a little bit better. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course C X

text:
Audio: A long time ago. Different, different environment. But a lot of of what we will do in this class has, you know, the same kind of underpinnings of the sciences that you will see in data science. I did my PhD at Carnegie Mellon, again, really long ago. My research has been in the study of parallel and distributed algorithms for statistical inference. We saw what it was. I came to Berkeley, 2015-2016, and since then, I've run a couple of major labs at Berkeley that work at the intersection of AI and systems. So my focus, if you can call that, is AI systems. I put links to a lot of different kinds of research projects I do on this slide. I will say my office hours, half my office hours, people come in and don't talk about the class at all. Instead, they talk about research, what's going on in Gen AI. I do a lot of work in Gen AI today. My lab runs some of the biggest open Gen AI programs in the world. So we run the systems that everyone now goes to. To figure out what is the best model today, so the chatbot arena. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course 0 X

text:
Audio: We run this software framework to serve. Most of the open source models in production. We also, in my group, do a lot of research in computer vision, interaction of Genai and kind of reasoning about things like human behavior. So a lot of really cool stuff the Genie Eye space. I also have some entrepreneurial activities outside of Berkeley, so I click out a few companies and my advisors to serve others working in AI and data science. And actually this on-error stuff that I've been doing has been a lot of fun because I got to speak to hundreds of data scientists in the past five years in a broad array of industries to understand what they are doing and what we are teaching and how it aligns. And the good news, I say, I can say confidence when we're teaching this class is very relevant to what people are doing in the real world today. Alright, so I also teach data 100. I taught the first one. In fact, they created the first data 100. It was a disaster. That was V0. V1 was also not great. V2 got better. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course 0 X

text:
Audio: And over time, it improved. I took a break and professionals know what they're doing took over the class, and it is now a much better class. So I got to teach again with Professor Norezzi last semester, and it has come a very long way. It is a well-organized, efficient class students learn, learn well and I generally enjoy the class so our teaching reviews have gone out substantially since the early days of day 100. text:
Audio: And also maybe where you are all are headed when you graduate. So when we started this journey of building a data science program at Berkeley. The hot things that data science were doing were transforming how people interacted with content, with advertising, all of Google, Facebook, I guess, Meta, Netflix, were driven by algorithms that were making decisions on what to see. Still true today, things like TikTok rely heavily on these algorithms, and that people designing these technologies are often the data scientists. So that's where we started. We had, in date eight, DJ Patel has a lecture. DJ is one of the people who actually coined the term, a scientist. He coined it to try to define the kind of scientific essence of what they were doing at Lincoln at the time, studying large amounts of data, trying to make decisions about who's, you should work for who, building connections in a big social graph. He was then the first data scientist for the US government who is hired by the Obama administration to run data science. And linked to the video lecture he gave, it's amazing. So data scientists became the first responders for the Obama administration. Something goes wrong, who are you gonna call? Got the Army? police, the data scientists. Why? Why would Obama pull a data scientist first? Video: 1: v

text:
Audio: Sparse data. signals from a black hole or signals around around a black hole. And then using ideas that we'll actually cover in this class. Deloping algorithms to take those signals and turn them into the first picture of a black hole. So the sparse recovery items that we will cover here are the same kinds of techniques being used to solve these problems. That's kind of neat about this is we have to use our parent knowledge because we don't have all the information. So this day was saying, you know, I assume black holes were actually snowmen. What would happen if I ran that actual image through these algorithms? And some algorithms, you know, have this paradox, it should be a hole, and they inject a hole in the picture even though there isn't one. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course 0 X

text:

Is that okay to use all this technology that really maybe could make a difference, maybe make better decisions, but also makes it harder to see what's going on that makes the I process more of it. As Dinoscience today, your job isn't just to make it a good decision, it's to communicate why that decision makes sense. This to provide clarity in these technologies. You're going to use things that are going to see opaque, which you need to be able to communicate. Why those things may help you make a decision you made. So talk about that in this class as well. The Ethics of Data Science, and These Technologies. But I signed in to the class anyways, I decided to push the program anyways. And that's because I'm very optimistic these technologies, these ideas are very empowering. So there's immense potential to address a challenge of facing society and you won't need those skills. So the future is in your hands and you will use this knowledge these skills for good. So that's why I am excited to teach data 100 and why the data 100 program I think has been very successful. Where the kind of neat things about the science differentiates it from a lot of the other engineering disciplines is it's also very human-centric. text:
Audio: How do I take a big set of data and understand? What it's telling me what it wants to tell me maybe what I should have asked with data missing. A lot of interview questions to have the flavor of his laptop with some interesting data on this folder coming something. You have 30 minutes. What do you do? do you go through that process? Data cleaning is a big part of data science technology is coming along. You'll do some data cleaning but the reality is that data is getting easier and easier to work with from the beginning. Which is great news. All this cool machine learning stuff, you know, 39% of people said they were doing some degree of, or I guess here, 36% of people said they were doing some degree of this machine learning work. on a daily basis. I think now you'll probably use more and more machine learning because it's become so much easier. So you'll see more of that and we'll cover that in this class. So a good question. Why is data cleaning less relevant? We'll get to more of that in the course, but short answer is that the technologies we use to manage data have come a long way and people become better at adopting more principal data management strategies for all of our data. You'll see I talk about the database side of the world too, I do some working in the database system, so no leak into the class. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course C X

About Data 100
Combining data, computation, and inferential thinking, data science is redefining how people and organizations
solve challenging problems and understand their world. This intermediate level class bridges between Data 8 and
Home / Schedule
upper division computer science and statistics courses as well as methods courses in other fields. In this class, we
Syllabus
explore key areas of data science including question formulation, data collection and cleaning, visualization,
statistical inference, predictive modeling, and decision making. Through a strong emphasis on data centric
Calendar
computing, quantitative critical thinking, and exploratory data analysis, this class covers key principles and
Resources
techniques of data science. These include languages for transforming, querying and analyzing data; algorithms for
Staff
machine learning methods including regression, classification and clustering; principles behind creating
Acknowledgments
informative data visualizations; statistical concepts of measurement error and prediction; and techniques for
scalable data processing.
Course Notes
Debugging Guide
Goals
Course FAQ
· Prepare students for advanced Berkeley courses in data-management, machine learning, and statistics, by
providing the necessary foundation and context.
· Enable students to start careers as data scientists by providing experience working with real-world data, tools,
and techniques.
.
Empower students to apply computational and inferential thinking to address real-world problems.
Prerequisites

Math: Linear Algebra (Math 54, EECS 16A, Math 56, Math 110, or Stat 89A). We will need some basic concepts
like linear operators, projections, and optimization to analyze and derive new prediction algorithms. This may
be satisfied concurrently to Data 100.

Course Culture
Students taking Data C100 come from a wide range of backgrounds. We hope to foster an inclusive and safe
learning environment based on curiosity rather than competition. All members of the course community - the
instructors, students, and course staff - are expected to treat each other with courtesy and respect. Some of the
responsibility for that lies with the staff, but a lot of it ultimately rests with you, the students.
Home / Schedule
Syllabus
Be Aware of Your Actions
Calendar
Sometimes, the little things add up to creating an unwelcoming culture to some students. For example, you and a
friend may think you are sharing a private joke about other races, majors, genders, abilities, cultures, etc. but this
Resources
can have adverse effects on classmates who overhear it. There is a great deal of research on something called
Staff
"stereotype threat": research finds that simply reminding someone that they belong to a particular culture or share
Acknowledgments
a particular identity (on whatever dimension) can interfere with their course performance.
Course Notes
Stereotype threat works both ways: you can assume that a student will struggle based on who they appear to be,
Debugging Guide
or you can assume that a student is doing great based on who they appear to be. Both are potentially harmful.
Course FAQ
Bear in mind that diversity has many facets, some of which are not visible. Your classmates may have medical
conditions (physical or mental), personal situations (financial, family, etc.), or interests that aren't common to most
students in the course. Another aspect of professionalism is avoiding comments that (likely unintentionally) put
down colleagues for situations they cannot control. Bragging in open space that an assignment is easy or "crazy,"
for example, can send subtle cues that discourage classmates who are dealing with issues that you can't see.
Please take care, so we can create a class in which all students feel supported and respected.

Beyond the slips that many of us make unintentionally are a host of behaviors that the course staff, department,

and university do not tolerate. These are generally classified under the term harassment; sexual harassment is a

specific form that is governed by federal laws known as Title IX.
Staff
NOTE
Acknowledgments
UC Berkeley's Title IX website provides many resources for understanding the terms, procedures, and policies

around harassment. Make sure you are aware enough of these issues to avoid crossing a line in your

interactions with other students. For example, repeatedly asking another student out on a date after they have
said no can cross this line.
Course FAQ
Your reaction to this topic might be to laugh it off, or to make or think snide remarks about "political correctness"
or jokes about consent or other things. You might think people just need to grow a thicker skin or learn to take a
joke. This isn't your decision to make. Research shows the consequences (emotional as well as physical) on people
who experience harassment. When your behavior forces another student to focus on something other than their
education, you have crossed a line. You have no right to take someone else's education away from them.

About Data 100
Combining data, computation, and inferential thinking, data science is redefining how people and organizations
solve challenging problems and understand their world. This intermediate level class bridges between Data 8 and
Home / Schedule
upper division computer science and statistics courses as well as methods courses in other fields. In this class, we
Syllabus
explore key areas of data science including question formulation, data collection and cleaning, visualization,
statistical inference, predictive modeling, and decision making. Through a strong emphasis on data centric
Calendar
computing, quantitative critical thinking, and exploratory data analysis, this class covers key principles and
Resources
techniques of data science. These include languages for transforming, querying and analyzing data; algorithms for
Staff
machine learning methods including regression, classification and clustering; principles behind creating
Acknowledgments
informative data visualizations; statistical concepts of measurement error and prediction; and techniques for
scalable data processing.
Course Notes
Debugging Guide
Goals
Course FAQ
· Prepare students for advanced Berkeley courses in data-management, machine learning, and statistics, by
providing the necessary foundation and context.
· Enable students to start careers as data scientists by providing experience working with real-world data, tools,
and techniques.
.
Empower students to apply computational and inferential thinking to address real-world problems.
Prerequisites

Foundations of Data Science: Data 8 covers much of the material in Data 100 but at an introductory level.

statistics, and machine learning.

.
Computing: The Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (CS 61A) or Computational Structures in
Data Science (Data 88C). These courses provide additional background in python programming (e.g ., for loops,
Debugging Guide
lambdas, debugging, and complexity) that will enable Data 100 to focus more on the concepts in Data Science
Course FAQ
and less on the details of programming in python.
Math: Linear Algebra (Math 54, EECS 16A, Math 56, Math 110, or Stat 89A). We will need some basic concepts
like linear operators, projections, and optimization to analyze and derive new prediction algorithms. This may
be satisfied concurrently to Data 100.

Course Culture
Students taking Data C100 come from a wide range of backgrounds. We hope to foster an inclusive and safe
learning environment based on curiosity rather than competition. All members of the course community - the
instructors, students, and course staff - are expected to treat each other with courtesy and respect. Some of the
responsibility for that lies with the staff, but a lot of it ultimately rests with you, the students.
Home / Schedule
Syllabus
Be Aware of Your Actions
Calendar
Sometimes, the little things add up to creating an unwelcoming culture to some students. For example, you and a
friend may think you are sharing a private joke about other races, majors, genders, abilities, cultures, etc. but this
Resources
can have adverse effects on classmates who overhear it. There is a great deal of research on something called
Staff
"stereotype threat": research finds that simply reminding someone that they belong to a particular culture or share
Acknowledgments
a particular identity (on whatever dimension) can interfere with their course performance.
Course Notes
Stereotype threat works both ways: you can assume that a student will struggle based on who they appear to be,
Debugging Guide
or you can assume that a student is doing great based on who they appear to be. Both are potentially harmful.
Course FAQ
Bear in mind that diversity has many facets, some of which are not visible. Your classmates may have medical
conditions (physical or mental), personal situations (financial, family, etc.), or interests that aren't common to most
students in the course. Another aspect of professionalism is avoiding comments that (likely unintentionally) put
down colleagues for situations they cannot control. Bragging in open space that an assignment is easy or "crazy,"
for example, can send subtle cues that discourage classmates who are dealing with issues that you can't see.
Please take care, so we can create a class in which all students feel supported and respected.

Course FAQ
Your reaction to this topic might be to laugh it off, or to make or think snide remarks about "political correctness"

who experience harassment. When your behavior forces another student to focus on something other than their
education, you have crossed a line. You have no right to take someone else's education away from them.

Data Science Student Climate

Data Science Undergraduate Studies faculty and staff are committed to creating a community where every person
feels respected, included, and supported. We recognize that incidents may happen, sometimes unintentionally,
Course FAQ
that run counter to this goal. There are many things we can do to try to improve the climate for students, but we
need to understand where the challenges lie. If you experience a remark, or disrespectful treatment, or if you feel
you are being ignored, excluded or marginalized in a course or program-related activity, please speak up. Consider
talking to your instructor, but you are also welcome to contact Executive Director Christina Teller at
cpteller@berkeley.edu or report an incident anonymously through this online form.

About Data 100
Combining data, computation, and inferential thinking, data science is redefining how people and organizations
solve challenging problems and understand their world. This intermediate level class bridges between Data 8 and
Home / Schedule
upper division computer science and statistics courses as well as methods courses in other fields. In this class, we
Syllabus
explore key areas of data science including question formulation, data collection and cleaning, visualization,
statistical inference, predictive modeling, and decision making. Through a strong emphasis on data centric
Calendar
computing, quantitative critical thinking, and exploratory data analysis, this class covers key principles and
Resources
techniques of data science. These include languages for transforming, querying and analyzing data; algorithms for
Staff
machine learning methods including regression, classification and clustering; principles behind creating
Acknowledgments
informative data visualizations; statistical concepts of measurement error and prediction; and techniques for
scalable data processing.
Course Notes
Debugging Guide
Goals
Course FAQ
· Prepare students for advanced Berkeley courses in data-management, machine learning, and statistics, by
providing the necessary foundation and context.
· Enable students to start careers as data scientists by providing experience working with real-world data, tools,
and techniques.
.
Empower students to apply computational and inferential thinking to address real-world problems.
Prerequisites

documentation theme for Jekyll.

DATA
100
Course Culture
Students taking Data C100 come from a wide range of backgrounds. We hope to foster an inclusive and safe
learning environment based on curiosity rather than competition. All members of the course community - the
instructors, students, and course staff - are expected to treat each other with courtesy and respect. Some of the
responsibility for that lies with the staff, but a lot of it ultimately rests with you, the students.
Home / Schedule
Syllabus
Be Aware of Your Actions
Calendar
Sometimes, the little things add up to creating an unwelcoming culture to some students. For example, you and a
friend may think you are sharing a private joke about other races, majors, genders, abilities, cultures, etc. but this
Resources
can have adverse effects on classmates who overhear it. There is a great deal of research on something called
Staff
"stereotype threat": research finds that simply reminding someone that they belong to a particular culture or share
Acknowledgments
a particular identity (on whatever dimension) can interfere with their course performance.
Course Notes
Stereotype threat works both ways: you can assume that a student will struggle based on who they appear to be,
Debugging Guide
or you can assume that a student is doing great based on who they appear to be. Both are potentially harmful.
Course FAQ
Bear in mind that diversity has many facets, some of which are not visible. Your classmates may have medical
conditions (physical or mental), personal situations (financial, family, etc.), or interests that aren't common to most
students in the course. Another aspect of professionalism is avoiding comments that (likely unintentionally) put
down colleagues for situations they cannot control. Bragging in open space that an assignment is easy or "crazy,"
for example, can send subtle cues that discourage classmates who are dealing with issues that you can't see.
Please take care, so we can create a class in which all students feel supported and respected.
This site uses Just the Docs, a
Be Respectful
documentation theme for Jekyll.

like linear operators, projections, and optimization to analyze and derive new prediction algorithms. This may
be satisfied concurrently to Data 100.
Please consult the Resources page for additional resources for reviewing prerequisite material.
Textbook: There is no official textbook for Data 100 this semester; we will provide course notes that will be

Course Culture
Students taking Data C100 come from a wide range of backgrounds. We hope to foster an inclusive and safe
learning environment based on curiosity rather than competition. All members of the course community - the
instructors, students, and course staff - are expected to treat each other with courtesy and respect. Some of the
responsibility for that lies with the staff, but a lot of it ultimately rests with you, the students.
Home / Schedule
Syllabus
Be Aware of Your Actions
Calendar
Sometimes, the little things add up to creating an unwelcoming culture to some students. For example, you and a
friend may think you are sharing a private joke about other races, majors, genders, abilities, cultures, etc. but this
Resources
can have adverse effects on classmates who overhear it. There is a great deal of research on something called
Staff
"stereotype threat": research finds that simply reminding someone that they belong to a particular culture or share
Acknowledgments
a particular identity (on whatever dimension) can interfere with their course performance.
Course Notes
Stereotype threat works both ways: you can assume that a student will struggle based on who they appear to be,
Debugging Guide
or you can assume that a student is doing great based on who they appear to be. Both are potentially harmful.
Course FAQ
Bear in mind that diversity has many facets, some of which are not visible. Your classmates may have medical
conditions (physical or mental), personal situations (financial, family, etc.), or interests that aren't common to most
students in the course. Another aspect of professionalism is avoiding comments that (likely unintentionally) put
down colleagues for situations they cannot control. Bragging in open space that an assignment is easy or "crazy,"
for example, can send subtle cues that discourage classmates who are dealing with issues that you can't see.
Please take care, so we can create a class in which all students feel supported and respected.

students. The staff of this course will treat you in a way that respects our differences. However, despite our best
efforts, we might slip up, hopefully inadvertently. If you are concerned about classroom environment issues
created by the staff or overall class dynamic, please feel free to talk to us about it. The instructors in particular
welcome any comments or concerns regarding conduct of the course and the staff. See below for how to best
reach us.

you are being ignored, excluded or marginalized in a course or program-related activity, please speak up. Consider
talking to your instructor, but you are also welcome to contact Executive Director Christina Teller at
cpteller@berkeley.edu or report an incident anonymously through this online form.

Audio: So all of these are going to be kind of released, meet day and Friday, depending on when everything is ready to be shipped. But then when their due is a little bit different. So labs are due on Tuesdays and homeworks and projects are due on Thursdays. So you have a little bit more time, but homeworks and projects because they're more substantial. And then you have less time with the labs because labs are expected to be completed in like one or two hours they come with a pre-recorded video voucher that you can just watch the video do the lab and then get ready to do the homework that's how they're structured do the lab first and then you should be able to do the homework using the information that you'll learn. So again the first lab and homework is going to be released tomorrow and then we're going to start this schedule pretty quickly. We also have a scattered office hours throughout the week. So we have office hours and Monday to give you help for the labs and then we also have office hours and Wednesday, Thursday and Friday. Moist to give help for the homework as well. We have discussion sections, so you have received information about discussion sections and I'm going to go over the logistics of discussion sections in a little bit, but you should have received a pre-semester form that you can decide whether you want to go to discussion or not And if you want to go to the discussion, what's your schedule? And then they'll allocate a specific discussion time. Either on Tuesday after the lectures or on Wednesday, I think we have the sessions scattered throughout the day on Wednesday. And obviously, the lectures are on Tuesday Thursday, and there will be a live from here. Lecture
There are 2 live lectures held on Tuesdays and Thursdays, 11:00am-12:30pm, hybrid: in-person in Wheeler 150 and
broadcasted live on Zoom. All session recordings, slides, activities, and examples will be uploaded to the course
Home / Schedule
website within 24 hours of the lecture.
Syllabus
Lecture participation: While synchronous lecture attendance (in-person or through Zoom) is not mandatory, we
Calendar
expect you to "participate" in lecture by answering lecture poll questions in one of two ways: (1) synchronously
during the lecture as poll questions are launched; or (2) asynchronously using a link provided after lecture.
Resources
Staff
· Lecture participation is graded using poll responses on a 0/1 basis.
Acknowledgments
· Synchronous Participation: complete at least one participation poll question during the live lecture timeslot
Course Notes
(11:00am-12:30pm, Tuesdays and Thursdays). As long as you submit a response to at least one poll question
Debugging Guide
in this timeframe, you will receive synchronous attendance credit.
· Asynchronous Participation: complete all participation poll questions from the link provided on the course
Course FAQ
website within one week of the corresponding lecture.
· In both cases, participation is graded on completion, not correctness.
· If you submit all participation polls over the course of the semester (i.e ., do not use any of the three automatic
drops), you will receive +0.5% bonus points applied to your final overall grade in the class.
· See the Policies section for lecture participation drops.
Discussion

documentation theme for Jekyll.

Syllabus
Lecture participation: While synchronous lecture attendance (in-person or through Zoom) is not mandatory, we
Calendar
expect you to "participate" in lecture by answering lecture poll questions in one of two ways: (1) synchronously
during the lecture as poll questions are launched; or (2) asynchronously using a link provided after lecture.
Resources
Staff
· Lecture participation is graded using poll responses on a 0/1 basis.
Acknowledgments
· Synchronous Participation: complete at least one participation poll question during the live lecture timeslot
Course Notes
(11:00am-12:30pm, Tuesdays and Thursdays). As long as you submit a response to at least one poll question
Debugging Guide
in this timeframe, you will receive synchronous attendance credit.
· Asynchronous Participation: complete all participation poll questions from the link provided on the course
Course FAQ
website within one week of the corresponding lecture.
· In both cases, participation is graded on completion, not correctness.
· If you submit all participation polls over the course of the semester (i.e ., do not use any of the three automatic
drops), you will receive +0.5% bonus points applied to your final overall grade in the class.
· See the Policies section for lecture participation drops.
Discussion

text:
Audio: So watch out for that. And then we'll also release the recordings, I think by the end of Tuesday and Thursday after each lecture. So that's where 5% of your horse grade is going to go. There is also anticipation that after you were sick, you were traveling, there was something coming up one week, and you missed one of the lectures, or a number of lectures, there is also a drop that was built in and you can use your drops if you miss any of those lectures. If you complete all of the lectures and you come to all of the lectures, there is a very small bonus percentage that you will receive. So if you're trying to collect all of those bonus points, this is a great opportunity for you. So discussions. As you saw on Ed, we have two discussion formats this semester. First of all, data 200 and 200 SN200A students, graduate students don't need to go to discussion. This only applies to undergraduate students, data 100. So there are two versions of grading for data 100. Grade at the session and ungraded the session. What the grade at the session, 5% of your your grade is for you to go to discussions, work on a worksheet with your TA and just is based on the participation or attendance to those discussions that you'll receive credit. There's also, I think, tree drops that we built in. I'm going to show you the exact numbers. Tree drops that built in that you can miss up to three discussions and still be able to get full credit on the discussion attendance. If you choose to not bother with the discussions, We're still expected to read the discussion worksheet and just know the problems that we've worked on for the final exam. problems that you solve and present as part of discussion are important in understanding course material.

students for homework assignments. It is to your benefit to actively participate in all discussions.

audio: So watch out for that. And then we'll also release the recordings, I think by the end of Tuesday and Thursday after each lecture. So that's where 5% of your horse grade is going to go. There is also anticipation that after you were sick, you were traveling, there was something coming up one week, and you missed one of the lectures, or a number of lectures, there is also a drop that was built in and you can use your drops if you miss any of those lectures. If you complete all of the lectures and you come to all of the lectures, there is a very small bonus percentage that you will receive. So if you're trying to collect all of those bonus points, this is a great opportunity for you. So discussions. As you saw on Ed, we have two discussion formats this semester. First of all, data 200 and 200 SN200A students, graduate students don't need to go to discussion. This only applies to undergraduate students, data 100. So there are two versions of grading for data 100. Grade at the session and ungraded the session. What the grade at the session, 5% of your your grade is for you to go to discussions, work on a worksheet with your TA and just is based on the participation or attendance to those discussions that you'll receive credit. There's also, I think, tree drops that we built in. I'm going to show you the exact numbers. Tree drops that built in that you can miss up to three discussions and still be able to get full credit on the discussion attendance. If you choose to not bother with the discussions, We're still expected to read the discussion worksheet and just know the problems that we've worked on for the final exam.

Lecture

Home / Schedule
website within 24 hours of the lecture.
Syllabus
Lecture participation: While synchronous lecture attendance (in-person or through Zoom) is not mandatory, we
Calendar
expect you to "participate" in lecture by answering lecture poll questions in one of two ways: (1) synchronously
during the lecture as poll questions are launched; or (2) asynchronously using a link provided after lecture.
Resources
Staff
· Lecture participation is graded using poll responses on a 0/1 basis.
Acknowledgments
· Synchronous Participation: complete at least one participation poll question during the live lecture timeslot
Course Notes
(11:00am-12:30pm, Tuesdays and Thursdays). As long as you submit a response to at least one poll question
Debugging Guide
in this timeframe, you will receive synchronous attendance credit.
· Asynchronous Participation: complete all participation poll questions from the link provided on the course
Course FAQ
website within one week of the corresponding lecture.
· In both cases, participation is graded on completion, not correctness.
If you submit all participation polls over the course of the semester (i.e ., do not use any of the three automatic
drops), you will receive +0.5% bonus points applied to your final overall grade in the class.
· See the Policies section for lecture participation drops.

Discussion
Live discussion sections are one hour long, and held on Tuesdays or Wednesdays. The goal of these TA-led
sessions is to work through problems, hone your skills, and flesh out your understanding as part of a team. The
Home / Schedule
problems that you solve and present as part of discussion are important in understanding course material.
Syllabus
The lectures, assignments, and exams of this course are structured with the expectation that all students
Calendar
complete the tasks in the discussion worksheet, either by attending section or on their own time. The
content covered in these sections is designed to solidify understanding of key lecture concepts and prepare
Resources
students for homework assignments. It is to your benefit to actively participate in all discussions.
Staff
Acknowledgments
IMPORTANT
Course Notes
This semester, we are offering two different grading schemes - graded versus ungraded discussion attendance.
Debugging Guide
Discussion sections will commence in the second week of class, and students will need to choose between the
two grading schemes, and these decisions will be final. Students who choose the Graded Discussion will be
Course FAQ
assigned a discussion attendance based on their availability. Please note that discussion attendance is not
mandatory for students enrolled in Data 200 and Data 200S.
If you are enrolled in Data 100, you have 2 options:
.
Graded Discussion: Discussion attendance will be recorded each week and account for 5% of the overall
grade. Each weekly discussion will be graded on a 0/1 basis. You will only get credit for attending the discussion

section that you are signed up for.

.

Audio: So it's still going to be within what's covered in the course. Somebody can use it as problem sets to get ready for the meet-term and final exam. But then that 5% is going to be reallocated to homeworks. So homeworks are going to have a higher percentage. So it, again, depends on how you like to manage your own time, whether you want to go to the sessions are not this very flexible. With the Pre-Sumester Survey, it's very important. The Pre-Sumester Survey is going to close tomorrow at noon. So you have to tell us whether you want to go to discussion or not go to discussion. If you choose that you don't want a discussion, discussion will not be assigned to you. You cannot opt in later. If you choose that you want a discussion, you'll assign a discussion to you. You can still go to the first week of this session. Say if you still want to continue or you want to opt up, opt out. So you have one week to opt out if you originally opt it. So you know, it's a live, complicated, but ask questions on it and will be here to help you. Video: 1: Y

sessions is to work through problems, hone your skills, and flesh out your understanding as part of a team. The
Home / Schedule
problems that you solve and present as part of discussion are important in understanding course material.
Syllabus
The lectures, assignments, and exams of this course are structured with the expectation that all students
Calendar
complete the tasks in the discussion worksheet, either by attending section or on their own time. The
content covered in these sections is designed to solidify understanding of key lecture concepts and prepare
Resources
students for homework assignments. It is to your benefit to actively participate in all discussions.
Staff
Acknowledgments
IMPORTANT
Course Notes
This semester, we are offering two different grading schemes - graded versus ungraded discussion attendance.
Debugging Guide
Discussion sections will commence in the second week of class, and students will need to choose between the
two grading schemes, and these decisions will be final. Students who choose the Graded Discussion will be
Course FAQ
assigned a discussion attendance based on their availability. Please note that discussion attendance is not
mandatory for students enrolled in Data 200 and Data 200S.
If you are enrolled in Data 100, you have 2 options:
.
Graded Discussion: Discussion attendance will be recorded each week and account for 5% of the overall
grade. Each weekly discussion will be graded on a 0/1 basis. You will only get credit for attending the discussion

sessions is to work through problems, hone your skills, and flesh out your understanding as part of a team. The
Home / Schedule
problems that you solve and present as part of discussion are important in understanding course material.
Syllabus
The lectures, assignments, and exams of this course are structured with the expectation that all students
Calendar
complete the tasks in the discussion worksheet, either by attending section or on their own time. The
content covered in these sections is designed to solidify understanding of key lecture concepts and prepare
Resources
students for homework assignments. It is to your benefit to actively participate in all discussions.
Staff
Acknowledgments
IMPORTANT
Course Notes
This semester, we are offering two different grading schemes - graded versus ungraded discussion attendance.

Discussion sections will commence in the second week of class, and students will need to choose between the
two grading schemes, and these decisions will be final. Students who choose the Graded Discussion will be

assigned a discussion attendance based on their availability. Please note that discussion attendance is not
mandatory for students enrolled in Data 200 and Data 200S.

Staff
the right part.
Acknowledgments
.
Homeworks and projects have both public (visible) and hidden autograder tests. The public tests are mainly
sanity checks. For example, a sanity check might verify that the answer you entered is a number as expected,
Course Notes
and not a word. The hidden tests generally check for correctness, and are invisible to students while they are
Debugging Guide
completing the assignment.

· The primary form of support students will have for homeworks and projects are office hours and Ed.

meant to be completed prior to homework.

Please refer to Grading Scheme for a comprehensive grade breakdown.
Homework and Projects
Homeworks are week-long assignments that are designed to help students develop an in-depth understanding of
Home / Schedule
both the theoretical and practical aspects of ideas presented in lecture. Projects are 2-week assignments (with a
Syllabus
weekly checkpoint) that synthesize multiple topics.
Calendar
· All homeworks and projects must be submitted to Gradescope by their posted deadlines. There may be
Resources
separate coding and written Gradescope portals for the same assignment; please check that you are submitting
Staff
the right part.
Acknowledgments
.
Homeworks and projects have both public (visible) and hidden autograder tests. The public tests are mainly
sanity checks. For example, a sanity check might verify that the answer you entered is a number as expected,
Course Notes
and not a word. The hidden tests generally check for correctness, and are invisible to students while they are
Debugging Guide
completing the assignment.
Course FAQ
· The primary form of support students will have for homeworks and projects are office hours and Ed.
Homeworks and projects must be completed individually, without the usage of any unauthorized resources
(CourseHero, ChatGPT, etc). See the Collaboration Policy for more details.
Lab
Labs are shorter, weekly programming assignments designed to give students familiarity with new ideas. They are
meant to be completed prior to homework.
This site uses Just the Docs, a
documentation theme for Jekyll.
· All lab assignments must be submitted to Gradescope by their posted deadlines.

All students enrolled in the graduate version of the course (CS C200A or Stat C200C, i.e. Data 200; Data 200S) will
be graded according to the Graduate grading scheme, which includes a team-based Graduate Final Project
distributed in the second half of the semester. More details to be in the coming weeks.

.

opportunities, and career planning.

On-Time Submission
All assignments are due at 11:59 PM Pacific Time on the due date specified on the Home / Schedule page. The
date and time of this deadline are firm. Submitting even a minute past is considered late.
Home / Schedule
Slip Days
Each student gets an extension budget of 12 total slip days for the 12 homeworks and projects (so use them
Syllabus
wisely). You can apply these slip days to homework and projects only (not labs) during the semester.
Calendar
Slip days are automatically applied based on the additional hours you take to submit any assignment after its
Resources
given deadline. Slip days are rounded up to the next day. For instance, 1 minute late counts as 1 day late. We will
Staff
use the submission time as displayed on Gradescope.
Acknowledgments
Course Notes
NOTE
Debugging Guide
If all 12 slip days are used for the first three homework assignments (for example), you are out of slip days and
cannot ask us to not consider one of the slip days previously used.
Course FAQ
Each project or homework can have a maximum of 4 slip days applied. After 4 days of the assignment due date, it
is unlikely that we will be able to accept your submission unless you have additional accommodations. Slip days
should be reserved for unforeseen circumstances. You should not plan to use your slip days regularly.
Additional Accommodations
We recognize that our students come from varied backgrounds and have widely-varying experiences. If you
This site uses Just the Docs, a
documentation theme for Jekyll.
require any additional accommodations any time in the semester, please do not hesitate to let us know. The
sooner we are made aware, the more options we have available for us to help you.

All assignments are due at 11:59 PM Pacific Time on the due date specified on the

Slip Days
Each student gets an extension budget of 12 total slip days for the 12 homeworks and projects (so use them

Syllabus
Slip days are automatically applied based on the additional hours you take to submit any assignment after its
Calendar
given deadline. Slip days are rounded up to the next day. For instance, 1 minute late counts as 1 day late. We will
Resources
use the submission time as displayed on Gradescope.
Staff
Acknowledgments
NOTE
If all 12 slip days are used for the first three homework assignments (for example), you are out of slip days and
Course Notes
cannot ask us to not consider one of the slip days previously used.
Debugging Guide
Course FAQ
Each project or homework can have a maximum of 4 slip days applied. After 4 days of the assignment due date, it
is unlikely that we will be able to accept your submission unless you have additional accommodations. Slip days
should be reserved for unforeseen circumstances. You should not plan to use your slip days regularly.

Audio: There was one more thing here that... If you require any additional accommodation, if there is anything that's happening that's beyond all of these slip days and drops and everything that's happening, we have a form that's called additional accommodation form. Fill it out and remember of our staff is going to be able to reach out to you within two business days. Don't expect a live staff responding to immediately. So wait for two business days and then they'll respond to your request. DSP accommodation. We have received most of the DSP accommodations that we're expecting. If you are expecting to get an accommodation, please have the DSP office send it to us ASAP so that we can calculate or be able to accommodate different pieces of your support. For DSP students, you're going to automatically get DSP students with assignment or homework extension accommodation. You're going to automatically get today's extension. Course Notes
space for conversation, as well as to arrange course/grading accommodations as necessary. For more information,
Debugging Guide
please email data 100.support@berkeley.edu.
Course FAQ
We recognize that at times, it can be difficult to manage your course performance - particularly at Berkeley's high
standards. Sometimes emergencies just come up (personal health emergency, family emergency, etc.). The
Additional Accommodations Form is meant to lower the barrier to reaching out to us, as well as build your
independence in managing your academic career long-term. So please do not hesitate to reach out.
Note that you cannot use the Additional Accommodations Form if you encounter a logistical oversight, such as

text:
Audio: If you need anything beyond two days, fill out the additional accommodation form and then we'll be able to help you figure out how we can accommodate your situation. For DSP students, we're not able to accommodate an extension beyond seven days. This is again because we're going to release solutions, so submitting a homework after that seven days is not practically possible, So again, reach out to us, we'll try to figure out how we can best navigate it. One more important fact that is new this semester is that for all of the students there is a requirement of Completing 50% of exams or the project. So, project applies to data 200, 200 S's students. But for everybody else, the average of your midterm and final exam, the average percent should be less greater than or equal to 50%. This is just to make sure that the amount of supervised work that you're doing in front of us in the Procure exam is reasonable with the whole chat GPT stuff that might go around for homeworks and last. So this is new semester. Historically, there shouldn't be an issue. Meet terms and final exams are pretty standard. You can see the historical trends. So this generally is not an issue for students. And should it not have been an issue if we had this policy prior to the semester as well. I think that's about it so I'm gonna go back to um and talk about different resources that we have in the course. So obviously you know the course website. The course website is at ds.org is slash fall 24. So you should be able to find all of the resources that we have there. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course O >

Each student gets an extension budget of 12 total slip days for the 12 homeworks and projects (so use them
Debugging Guide
wisely). You can apply these slip days to homework and projects only (not labs) during the semester.
Course FAQ
Slip days are automatically applied based on the additional hours you take to submit any assignment after its
given deadline. Slip days are rounded up to the next day. For instance, 1 minute late counts as 1 day late. We will
use the submission time as displayed on Gradescope.
NOTE
If all 12 slip days are used for the first three homework assignments (for example), you are out of slip days and
cannot ask us to not consider one of the slip days previously used.

To pass this course, students must achieve a total weighted average percentage of at least 50% across the

midterm and final exams. This means that even if a student excels in other components of the course, a total

weighted average below 50% on these exams will result in a failing grade for the course.

Acknowledgments

text:
Audio: The programming environment that you're using is Jupyter Lab. It's similar to Jupyter notebooks that you'd seen in Data Aid, but now it has all of these file management area that you have here and you should be able to load your data, take a look at your data, and be able to create files, and then manage kind of how you want to structure everything that you have. And then you can also have kind of a mini computer thing that you can have multiple windows and then be able to see what's going on in the notebook that you're working on at that given point in time. If you are in data 200, 200, 800, 800 s and you haven't seen Jupyter Notebooks before, these are some good resources for you that you can quickly go over and then get used to how this infrastructure is going to work out in data 100. In Lab 1 you will actually learn a bunch of shortcuts on how to professionally use Jupyter Lab so that's going to be a really great resource for you and that's going to be your Lab 1. So just learning how to play with Jupyter Lab. So now I'm going to turn over and talk a little bit about data science life cycle. Um, because we're going to go over this data science life cycle and then see different elements of the course from the perspective of which stage of data science life cycle were in. Is there a question? Yeah. Question is, what's the difference between Jupyter Notebook and Jupyter Lab? I would summarize it as Jupyter Notebook is a simplified version of Jupyter Lab for beginners. So in Jupyter Notebook, you're not going to mess around with files and managing everything yourself. So there's less functionality. So you have like a plain notebook that you can run through cells and write text cell and put cell and whatnot. But Jupyter Lab, you have more functionality available to you. As I said, like mostly that file system thing, managing all the windows that you can see and you will have a terminal in the same kind of environment so you should be able to also use command line if you need. audio: The programming environment that you're using is Jupyter Lab. It's similar to Jupyter notebooks that you'd seen in Data Aid, but now it has all of these file management area that you have here and you should be able to load your data, take a look at your data, and be able to create files, and then manage kind of how you want to structure everything that you have. And then you can also have kind of a mini computer thing that you can have multiple windows and then be able to see what's going on in the notebook that you're working on at that given point in time. If you are in data 200, 200, 800, 800 s and you haven't seen Jupyter Notebooks before, these are some good resources for you that you can quickly go over and then get used to how this infrastructure is going to work out in data 100. In Lab 1 you will actually learn a bunch of shortcuts on how to professionally use Jupyter Lab so that's going to be a really great resource for you and that's going to be your Lab 1. So just learning how to play with Jupyter Lab. So now I'm going to turn over and talk a little bit about data science life cycle. Um, because we're going to go over this data science life cycle and then see different elements of the course from the perspective of which stage of data science life cycle were in. Is there a question? Yeah. Question is, what's the difference between Jupyter Notebook and Jupyter Lab? I would summarize it as Jupyter Notebook is a simplified version of Jupyter Lab for beginners. So in Jupyter Notebook, you're not going to mess around with files and managing everything yourself. So there's less functionality. So you have like a plain notebook that you can run through cells and write text cell and put cell and whatnot. But Jupyter Lab, you have more functionality available to you. As I said, like mostly that file system thing, managing all the windows that you can see and you will have a terminal in the same kind of environment so you should be able to also use command line if you need.

text:
Audio: Okay. What is data science lifecycle? So imagine if you go ahead and Google data science lifecycle and then figure out like what data science lifecycle is and you're going to get all of these colorful flow charts and whatnot. Most of them are correct. Most of them are explaining the life cycle that any data scientist or data engineer is going through on a daily basis or in a project. But what we're going to work on in data 100 is remembering this workflow. This is a data science life cycle workflow in data 100, which is kind of a high level description of a data science workflow. So the way that this workflow works is that it has two entry points. So you see these two entry points right here. So a project generally for data scientists would start either when you have a question. And you have a research question and then the next step for you is to go and find the data, obtain the data that is stage two right there, or that you have an stakeholder that is going to present a data to you and then say that, oh, we have this data, would you be able to extract this information for us or help us analyze this data? That's a second entry point. That you don't have a research question, you might want to ask your words, go ahead and formulate a question, but then the initial work list starts with obtaining the data. And then it goes around the cycle, you can see that there are arrows forward and backward. And this kind of shows that this life cycle is pretty iterative. And it's kind of very important for us that everything that we teach in data 100 we're going to tell you which stage we're in. And it will make sense to you why we're going around the cycle and why this cycle is actually. Video: 1: Lec 01 - DS100 Fa24 - Course 0 X

audio: Okay. What is data science lifecycle? So imagine if you go ahead and Google data science lifecycle and then figure out like what data science lifecycle is and you're going to get all of these colorful flow charts and whatnot. Most of them are correct. Most of them are explaining the life cycle that any data scientist or data engineer is going through on a daily basis or in a project. But what we're going to work on in data 100 is remembering this workflow. This is a data science life cycle workflow in data 100, which is kind of a high level description of a data science workflow. So the way that this workflow works is that it has two entry points. So you see these two entry points right here. So a project generally for data scientists would start either when you have a question. And you have a research question and then the next step for you is to go and find the data, obtain the data that is stage two right there, or that you have an stakeholder that is going to present a data to you and then say that, oh, we have this data, would you be able to extract this information for us or help us analyze this data? That's a second entry point. That you don't have a research question, you might want to ask your words, go ahead and formulate a question, but then the initial work list starts with obtaining the data. And then it goes around the cycle, you can see that there are arrows forward and backward. And this kind of shows that this life cycle is pretty iterative. And it's kind of very important for us that everything that we teach in data 100 we're going to tell you which stage we're in. And it will make sense to you why we're going around the cycle and why this cycle is actually.

text:
Audio: So this is the part that you will calculate whether your data is enough and whether your data is in the right format for you to do the modeling and move onto the modeling phase. This is not modeling yet. And then understanding the word is the part that we have modeling and modeling which is prediction and inference. In a prediction task we would design a model that then we can basically use that model in similar settings and similar datasets and be able to figure out how the model is performing and how we can predict on the same task. In France is a slightly different. So it's kind of taking one step further and instead of just doing prediction on the same path, you're doing the inference on relevant path and looking at the implication of your prediction on other domains. Data 102 is gonna be the next course, to data 100 which covers more about inference. In data 100, we're gonna see a lot of modeling that we'll be using, clustering, regression, classification and things like that, which is in the area of prediction paths. Principles and
Techniques of Data
Data science is an interdisciplinary field with a variety of applications and offers
Science
great potential to address challenging societal issues. By building data science
skills, you can empower yourself to participate in and drive conversations that
shape your life and society as a whole, whether that be fighting against climate
change, launching diversity initiatives, or more.
Welcome
The field of data science is rapidly evolving; many of the key technical
1 Introduction
underpinnings in modern-day data science have been popularized during the
2 Pandas I
early 21st century, and you will learn them throughout the course. It has a wide
3 Pandas II
range of applications from science and medicine to sports.
4 Pandas III
5 Data Cleaning and EDA
While data science has immense potential to address challenging problems facing
6 Regular Expressions
society by enhancing our critical thinking, it can also be used obscure complex
7 Visualization I
decisions and reinforce historical trends and biases. This course will implore you
8 Visualization II
to consider the ethics of data science within its applications.
9 Sampling
10 Introduction to
Data science is fundamentally human-centered and facilitates decision-making by
Modeling
quantitatively balancing tradeoffs. To quantify things reliably, we must use and
11 Constant Model, Loss,
and Transformations
analyze data appropriately, apply critical thinking and skepticism at every step of
12 Ordinary Least Squares
the way, and consider how our decisions affect others.

1.2 Conclusion
Principles and
Techniques of Data
Data science is an interdisciplinary field with a variety of applications and offers
Science
great potential to address challenging societal issues. By building data science
skills, you can empower yourself to participate in and drive conversations that
shape your life and society as a whole, whether that be fighting against climate
change, launching diversity initiatives, or more.
Welcome
The field of data science is rapidly evolving; many of the key technical
1 Introduction
underpinnings in modern-day data science have been popularized during the
2 Pandas I
early 21st century, and you will learn them throughout the course. It has a wide
3 Pandas II
range of applications from science and medicine to sports.
4 Pandas III
5 Data Cleaning and EDA
While data science has immense potential to address challenging problems facing
6 Regular Expressions
society by enhancing our critical thinking, it can also be used obscure complex
7 Visualization I
decisions and reinforce historical trends and biases. This course will implore you
8 Visualization II
to consider the ethics of data science within its applications.
9 Sampling
10 Introduction to
Data science is fundamentally human-centered and facilitates decision-making by
Modeling
quantitatively balancing tradeoffs. To quantify things reliably, we must use and
11 Constant Model, Loss,
and Transformations
analyze data appropriately, apply critical thinking and skepticism at every step of
12 Ordinary Least Squares
the way, and consider how our decisions affect others.

Principles and
Techniques of Data
Data science is an interdisciplinary field with a variety of applications and offers
Science
great potential to address challenging societal issues. By building data science
skills, you can empower yourself to participate in and drive conversations that
shape your life and society as a whole, whether that be fighting against climate
change, launching diversity initiatives, or more.
Welcome
The field of data science is rapidly evolving; many of the key technical
1 Introduction
underpinnings in modern-day data science have been popularized during the
2 Pandas I
early 21st century, and you will learn them throughout the course. It has a wide
3 Pandas II
range of applications from science and medicine to sports.
4 Pandas III
5 Data Cleaning and EDA
While data science has immense potential to address challenging problems facing
6 Regular Expressions
society by enhancing our critical thinking, it can also be used obscure complex
7 Visualization I
decisions and reinforce historical trends and biases. This course will implore you
8 Visualization II
to consider the ethics of data science within its applications.
9 Sampling
10 Introduction to
Data science is fundamentally human-centered and facilitates decision-making by
Modeling
quantitatively balancing tradeoffs. To quantify things reliably, we must use and
11 Constant Model, Loss,
and Transformations
analyze data appropriately, apply critical thinking and skepticism at every step of
12 Ordinary Least Squares
the way, and consider how our decisions affect others.

text:
Audio: So code is gonna spin up a data 100, data hub for you, and then you should be able to actually run stuff. Code HTML, if you're on your phone, and you just wanna take a look at the code that be posted, you should be able to do that as well. So I'm going to switch here. And walk you through one simple example of data science life cycle. So this simple example of data science life cycle is that what I want is that I have a bunch of different questions. So the questions that I have is that I'll try to answer through this demo is that I want to see how many students we have in the course. I'm sure you're all curious to see how big this this offering is. And then we're going to see whether the majors, whether the top majors, what's the distribution between grad students, undergrad students, the distribution of names. all the fun stuff that you'll get to know about data 100. And then the last thing is that we're gonna take a look at the trend of the distribution of undergrad and grad students on campus historically since 2013 and the distribution of undergrad and grads in our course and just compared with our trends to see if we're rather than the same, if we're like representative of this trend that we have. Audio: So what I'll do is I will load majors that CSV in a data frame called majors and names that CSV in a data frame called Names. And then we'll basically take a look at the data and try to understand the data. So this is how the flow of processing data or responding to one data science posture looks like. how do I start understanding the data? What I can do is I can just take a peek at the data. So head is going to give me the top x number of rows. So here I'm saying that head 20, give me the top 20 rows in this spreadsheet. And again, because they are randomly kind of ordered, this is just going to give me the top 20 kind of majors that we have. So you're seeing that, okay, the person that was represented in the first row is from letters and sciences, undeclared, so on and so forth. So you should be able to see that in majors, data frame, this is a data structure. You have majors and I have terms and attendance. So that's why we take a look at the data. We want to see attributes, we want to see how many rows we have, and then we want to generally see the structure to save this structure is within what we're expecting or not.
